title: Today's AI Brief: Gemini 3.5 and Strava's API Shift
summary: Google's Gemini 3.5 model and Strava's API access changes take center stage in AI news.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI brief. Today, we're looking at some fresh developments in the world of artificial intelligence. Google's DeepMind team has just unveiled Gemini 3.5, their latest model that's supposed to handle complex tasks with ease. Meanwhile, Strava is tightening its API access, and it's got some interesting implications for developers and AI apps. And, as a bonus, I'll also touch on what these changes mean for us.

First up, Google's Gemini 3.5 model is making waves. According to the blog post, it's designed to help execute complex workflows, which sounds like a fancy way of saying it can handle a lot of tasks at once. The interesting part here is that it's built to be more 'agentic,' meaning it can take actions on its own, rather than just providing information. This could have huge implications for automation and productivity – imagine having a virtual assistant that can actually take care of tasks for you, without needing constant supervision.

Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Gemini 3.5: frontier intelligence with action May 19, 2026 · Share x.com Facebook LinkedIn Mail Copy link Gemini 3.5 is built to help you execute complex, agentic workflows. Koray Kavukcuoglu CTO, Google DeepMind and Chief AI Architect, Google Jeff Dean Chief Scientist, Google DeepMind and Google Research Oriol Vinyals Vice President, Google DeepMind Noam S... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Gadgets AI News Strava blames zero-code AI apps and scrapers as it tightens API access Developers will now have to pay a $11.99 / month subscription to build apps using Strava’s data. Developers will now have to pay a $11.99 / month subscription to build apps using Strava’s data. by Emma Roth Jun 1, 2026, 2:06 PM UTC Image: Cath Virginia / The Verge Emma Ro... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
